title: `wait` can hang forever on missing or expired windows and never sends an IPC response

evidence.quote: if (!w || w.state !== 'open') throw new Error(`Window ${ref} is not open.`);

reasoning: Unlike the mutating commands, `wait` never validates the window reference. If the window does not exist, has expired from the registry, or is already closed and its queue is empty, `registry.resolve(window)?.queue.read(type)` stays falsy forever. With no timeout provided, the interval and open IPC request live indefinitely, so the caller hangs instead of getting the same invalid-window error that other commands return.
reproduction: Call `wait` on a nonexistent window ID, or on a closed window after its final event has already been consumed, without `--timeout`. The command never returns.
recommendation: Validate the target window up front for `wait`, or terminate the wait when the window is missing/closed and return a structured error instead of polling forever. Keep timeout as an upper bound, not the only escape hatch.
whyTestsDoNotAlreadyCoverThis: No test exercises daemon IPC behavior for invalid window references; the existing suite stops at `EventQueue` unit tests.
suggestedRegressionTest: An integration test that sends `wait` for an unknown window and asserts a prompt error response, plus a test for waiting on a closed/expired window after consuming `window.closed`.
minimumFixScope: src/daemon/daemon.ts, with any needed registry helper changes in src/daemon/window-registry.ts
